museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.curatorNote: Initial researched draft for June 1995. Treat precise dates as source-noted; broad market atmosphere is marked as context.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: Five researched moments or context markers, with cautious wording where the month is a quiet drawer.

items.title: E3 coverage reaches UK readers through magazines.
items.body: From a UK perspective, this mattered through retail timing, import pages, playground talk and the monthly magazine cycle.
items.sourceNote: See source notes for this exhibit; month-specific claims are separated from broader period context.

items.title: Saturn's Western story is complicated by the early US launch.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: Eight notable games from the year, led by month-specific anchors when the evidence supports them.

items.title: Chrono Trigger
items.body: Square's dream-team RPG becomes one of the late 16-bit era's defining objects.
items.sourceNote: Japanese release date is widely cited.

items.title: Descent
items.body: Parallax's six-degrees shooter makes PC movement feel newly disorienting.
items.sourceNote: 1995 release is secure; exact month varies by source.

items.title: Full Throttle
items.body: LucasArts turns biker pulp into a compact, cinematic adventure.
items.sourceNote: April 1995 release is commonly cited.

items.title: Super Mario World 2: Yoshi's Island
items.body: Nintendo gives the SNES a hand-drawn late-era flourish.
items.sourceNote: Japanese August 1995 release is widely cited.

items.title: Command & Conquer
items.body: Westwood gives RTS a louder, more cinematic command structure.
items.sourceNote: September 1995 release is widely cited.

items.title: Wipeout
items.body: Psygnosis ties PlayStation racing to UK club culture and design.
items.sourceNote: European launch-title context is widely cited.

items.title: Donkey Kong Country 2: Diddy's Kong Quest
items.body: Rare keeps the SNES visually alive in the PlayStation year.
items.sourceNote: November 1995 release context is widely cited.

items.title: Warcraft II: Tides of Darkness
items.body: Blizzard's RTS sequel sharpens the PC strategy war.
items.sourceNote: December 1995 release is widely cited.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: Machines, formats and buying context around the exhibit month.

items.title: Sega Saturn reaches Europe
items.body: Sega's 32-bit machine arrives first, but with a complicated price, timing and software story.
items.sourceNote: European Saturn launch is commonly cited as July 1995.

items.title: PlayStation reaches Europe
items.body: Sony enters UK high streets with a clearer brand, CD cases and launch games that feel club-adjacent.
items.sourceNote: Computing History cites September 29, 1995 for Europe.

items.title: Virtual Boy is shown at E3
items.body: Nintendo's red tabletop experiment becomes a warning sign that not every future is comfortable.
items.sourceNote: E3 1995 source context.

items.title: Pentium PCs and CD-ROM drives become desirable
items.body: Command & Conquer, Descent and multimedia titles make PC upgrades feel less optional.
items.sourceNote: 1995 PC context.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: How players found information before search, streams and social feeds.

items.title: The web starts appearing in magazine margins
items.body: URLs begin to feel like little doorways, though most players still rely on print.

items.title: RTS players talk tactics by modem and LAN
items.body: Command & Conquer helps make multiplayer strategy part of PC social life.

items.title: Demo culture remains physical
items.body: PlayStation demo discs and PC cover discs are still the main way many UK players sample the future.

items.title: Early fan pages feel handmade
items.body: The games web is present but scattered, closer to a noticeboard than a polished archive.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: A short atmospheric reading of the month from the player side of history.

items.title: The future came in a jewel case
items.body: PlayStation made games feel closer to music, club culture and electronics retail than toy shelves.

items.title: Sega arrived first but noisier
items.body: Saturn had arcade credibility, but the story around it was harder for ordinary buyers to parse.

items.title: PC strategy had a new command voice
items.body: Command & Conquer turned base-building into a language of barks, clicks and green video briefings.

items.title: Christmas felt expensive
items.body: A new console, a CD-ROM drive or one more SNES cartridge could all plausibly be the right answer.
